past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 12 years ago, well-controlled on oral agents
family_history: Mother had Alzheimer's disease diagnosed at age 75, died at age 82
Father had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died of stroke at age 71
One brother, age 70, has mild cognitive complaints but not evaluated
Maternal aunt had dementia, type unknown

social_history.education: High school diploma, completed some community college courses
social_history.living_situation: Lives with his wife of 50 years in a two-story house; adult daughter lives nearby
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 15 pack-year history, quit 20 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, one to two beers on weekends
social_history.exercise: Used to walk the neighborhood daily, has become less motivated over the past year

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place; knows month and year but states the date as three days prior. MMSE 21/30, losing points on recall (0/3 at 5 minutes), orientation (2 points lost), attention/serial sevens (3 errors), and copy of intersecting pentagons (incomplete). Clock drawing test shows all numbers present but bunched to the right side of the clock face, with hands set to an incorrect time. Unable to recall any of the 3 registered words after 5 minutes, even with phonemic cues. Naming intact for common objects but unable to name the stem of a watch or the eraser on a pencil. Repetition intact. Follows two-step commands but has difficulty with three-step commands. Judgment mildly impaired on hypothetical scenarios.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation. Extraocular movements full bilaterally without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Facial sensation intact. Face symmetric with normal strength. Hearing mildly diminished bilaterally, age-appropriate. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone in all extremities. No rigidity. Strength 5/5 throughout. No pronator drift. No tremor, chorea, or myoclonus.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch and pinprick in all extremities. Vibration mildly diminished at bilateral great toes, otherwise intact. Proprioception intact at the toes bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady with normal base and stride. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. No festination, no shuffling.
neurological_exam.additional: No paratonic rigidity. No grasp or palmomental reflexes. No snout reflex. No myoclonus.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive memory problems over two years, getting noticeably worse in the last eight months
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Priya Sharma is a 69-year-old right-handed retired postal worker who is brought to the outpatient neurology clinic by her wife, Dorothy, for evaluation of progressive memory difficulties. Mrs. Jenkins reports that she first noticed something was wrong approximately two years ago when her husband began misplacing her car keys and wallet repeatedly, something she had never done before. She initially attributed it to normal aging, but over the past eight months the problems have become much more apparent and concerning. She now frequently asks the same questions multiple times within a single conversation, sometimes within minutes, and does not seem to recall that she already asked. She has forgotten several scheduled appointments with her primary care physician and her barber despite having kept these routines reliably for decades. Last Thanksgiving, she could not remember the name of her grandson's wife, whom she has known for six years, causing considerable embarrassment and family concern. Her daughter reports that when she calls to tell him about upcoming family plans, she has no recollection of the conversation the following day. She was previously an avid reader of history books but has stopped reading in the past year because she says she cannot remember what happened in previous chapters. She attempted to help her wife with preparing their tax documents this past winter, a task she had always managed independently, but became confused by the forms and gave up after making several errors. Her wife has since taken over managing their finances. She has also had difficulty operating the new television remote control and the microwave, despite being shown how to use them on multiple occasions. She got lost driving home from the hardware store two months ago, a route she has taken hundreds of times over thirty years, and had to call her wife for directions. Since then, the family has asked him not to drive alone. She continues to dress and bathe independently, though her wife notes she has worn the same outfit two days in a row on several occasions without realizing it. She denies any hallucinations, and her wife confirms there have been no episodes of seeing things that are not there. There have been no significant personality or behavioral changes, though she has become somewhat more withdrawn and less interested in socializing with friends from her lodge. She denies headaches, seizures, falls, tremor, or difficulty walking. Her sleep has been unremarkable with no acting out of dreams reported by her wife.

signal_characteristics.T1: Bilateral hippocampal volume loss with prominent choroidal fissures and widened temporal horns, left slightly more pronounced than right
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No abnormal signal within hippocampal formations

signal_characteristics.Coronal_T1: Enlarged CSF spaces around medial temporal structures bilaterally, consistent with significant parenchymal volume loss

signal_characteristics.T1: Prominent sulcal widening in bilateral temporal and parietal convexities disproportionate to the patient's stated age

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Confluent periventricular hyperintensities and scattered deep white matter hyperintensities, consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy, Scheltens MTA score 3 bilaterally
volumetrics.ventricular_system: Moderate lateral ventriculomegaly, predominantly ex vacuo in nature secondary to parenchymal volume loss, with mild prominence of the third ventricle
volumetrics.cortical_thickness: Reduced cortical thickness in temporal and parietal association areas

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or space-occupying lesions identified
No evidence of hemorrhage on SWI sequences
No abnormal meningeal enhancement on post-contrast sequences
Two small old lacunar infarcts in the left basal ganglia, likely incidental
mri.impression: Bilateral hippocampal and medial temporal lobe atrophy (Scheltens MTA grade 3) with temporoparietal-predominant cortical volume loss. The degree of atrophy is disproportionate to the patient's age. Moderate chronic small vessel ischemic changes (Fazekas grade 2), likely related to the patient's hypertension and diabetes history. Two old lacunar infarcts in the left basal ganglia. No acute intracranial pathology. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 149 mEq/L (H), Chloride 111 mEq/L (H), Glucose 113 mg/dL (H), HbA1c 6.9 % (H).

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall
Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: MMSE 21/30 points (H), MoCA 16/30 points (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall 3rd percentile percentile (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall < 1st percentile percentile (H), Boston Naming Test 18th percentile percentile (H), Category Fluency (Animals) 8th percentile percentile (H), Letter Fluency (FAS) 22nd percentile percentile (H), Trail Making Test Part B 12th percentile percentile (H), Rey Complex Figure - Copy 20th percentile percentile (H), Geriatric Depression Scale 5/15 points (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Trail Making Test Part B: 12th percentile percentile (H)

findings.location: Diffuse cortical uptake involving prefrontal, lateral temporal, parietal, and precuneus/posterior cingulate cortex

signal_characteristics.Distribution: Widespread bilateral cortical amyloid binding with relative sparing of sensorimotor and occipital cortex

additional_observations: Non-specific white matter uptake within expected range
No focal asymmetry to suggest atypical AD variant
output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. Widespread cortical amyloid deposition with elevated cortical-to-cerebellar SUVr of 1.55, indicating moderate-to-high amyloid burden. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Bilateral temporoparietal cortex, posterior cingulate, and precuneus

signal_characteristics.FDG_PET: Markedly reduced FDG uptake in bilateral temporal and parietal association cortices with involvement of the posterior cingulate gyrus
signal_characteristics.Pattern: Classic temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism with preserved metabolism in primary sensorimotor cortex, primary visual cortex, basal ganglia, thalamus, and cerebellum

additional_observations: Mild frontal hypometabolism beginning to emerge, suggesting possible progression beyond earliest stage
Basal ganglia and thalamic metabolism within normal limits
output.impression: See findings above. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 15 cmH2O. WBC: 2 cells/uL (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 43.3 mg/dL. Glucose: 64.6 mg/dL (ratio 0.58 (serum glucose 118 mg/dL)). Amyloid_beta_42: 342 pg/mL (reference >500 pg/mL) - DECREASED. Amyloid_beta_40: 7800 pg/mL (reference range 5000-12000 pg/mL) - Normal. Amyloid_beta_42_40_ratio: 0.044 (reference >0.065) - DECREASED. Phosphorylated_tau_181: 88 pg/mL (reference <60 pg/mL) - ELEVATED. Total_tau: 520 pg/mL (reference <400 pg/mL) - ELEVATED. 14-3-3_protein: Negative. RT-QuIC: Negative.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-7.5 Hz alpha activity over the occipital regions, below the expected normal range of 8.5-12 Hz
background.reactivity: PDR attenuates with eye opening, though response is somewhat sluggish

findings.morphology: Continuous low-amplitude theta intermixed with background

artifacts.impact: Minimal, did not obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient age
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photoparoxysmal response; photic driving present at low frequencies only

output.impression: Mildly to moderately abnormal EEG. The posterior dominant rhythm is slowed at 7-7.5 Hz with diffuse excess theta activity and bitemporal predominance. No epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures were recorded. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Hyperventilation was not performed. Recording limited to 30 minutes of wakefulness.

interactions: No significant interactions with metformin, amlodipine, losartan, rosuvastatin, acetaminophen, or aspirin
warnings: May cause bradycardia; baseline heart rate of 76 bpm is reassuring but periodic monitoring is recommended
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, anorexia) are common upon initiation and are usually transient
Avoid concurrent anticholinergic medications as they may counteract the therapeutic effect
Monitor for weight loss which may affect diabetes management
alternatives: Rivastigmine transdermal patch 4.6 mg/24 hr - may have fewer GI side effects
Galantamine ER 8 mg daily - alternative cholinesterase inhibitor

differential.key_distinguishing: Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease and old lacunar infarcts suggest possible vascular contribution; however, the predominantly amnestic profile and hippocampal atrophy pattern favor AD as the primary pathology
Gradual progressive decline rather than stepwise; memory-predominant rather than executive-predominant; hippocampal atrophy pattern on MRI is more characteristic of AD than pure vascular dementia
Gradual onset over 2 years with insidious progression; GDS 5/15 indicates at most mild reactive symptoms; cognitive deficits are far more severe than mood symptoms would explain
No visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism on examination, no REM sleep behavior disorder, no fluctuating cognition
No prominent behavioral or personality changes; memory impairment predominates over executive and language deficits; social decorum preserved

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and reliable informant including timeline of cognitive changes, nature of deficits, functional decline, and behavioral symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive amnestic decline over 2 years with significant functional impairment confirmed by wife and daughter

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with detailed mental status assessment including standardized screening (MMSE or MoCA)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: MMSE 21/30 with prominent recall deficit (0/3); otherwise normal neurological examination

optimal_actions.action: Order dementia screening labs: CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate, RPR, HIV
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal results except mildly elevated glucose and HbA1c consistent with known diabetes; reversible causes of dementia excluded

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 3) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss; Fazekas grade 2 white matter changes
Multidomain amnestic dementia profile with severely impaired delayed recall, reduced semantic fluency and naming, impaired executive function

optimal_actions.action: Consider CSF biomarkers or amyloid PET for pathological confirmation of Alzheimer disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Decreased Abeta42, decreased Abeta42/40 ratio, elevated phospho-tau and total tau (A+T+N+ profile)

optimal_actions.action: Optimize vascular risk factor management given comorbid diabetes, hypertension, and small vessel disease on MRI
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Review and optimize glycemic control, blood pressure management, and statin therapy

optimal_actions.action: Initiate cholinesterase inhibitor therapy (donepezil 5 mg daily, titrate to 10 mg after 4-6 weeks if tolerated)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions with current medications

optimal_actions.action: Provide diagnosis disclosure and counseling to patient and family, including prognosis, safety planning, driving cessation, advance directives, and community resources
Schedule follow-up in 3-6 months to monitor treatment response, cognitive trajectory, and functional status

critical_actions: Exclude reversible causes of dementia with laboratory testing (TSH, B12, RPR, HIV)
Perform standardized cognitive assessment (MMSE, MoCA, or formal neuropsychological battery)
Obtain collateral history from a reliable informant
Assess functional status and safety including driving capacity
Address vascular risk factors (diabetes, hypertension) given comorbid small vessel disease
Discuss diagnosis, prognosis, and safety planning with patient and family
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., diphenhydramine, oxybutynin) which worsen cognitive function in dementia
Prescribing benzodiazepines without careful consideration of fall risk and cognitive side effects
Attributing symptoms to normal aging without performing formal evaluation
Starting memantine monotherapy for mild-stage disease (not indicated until moderate-to-severe stage)
Ordering brain biopsy when clinical presentation and biomarkers are concordant
Prescribing antipsychotics without a clear behavioral indication given increased mortality risk in dementia patients
Continuing to allow unsupervised driving given documented episodes of getting lost on familiar routes
key_reasoning_points: Progressive episodic memory loss over 2 years with insidious onset and gradual worsening is the hallmark presentation of Alzheimer disease
Functional decline (inability to manage finances, getting lost on familiar routes, inability to learn new devices) establishes dementia rather than mild cognitive impairment
MMSE 21/30 with complete failure on delayed recall (0/3) despite cueing is characteristic of hippocampal-type amnesia in AD
Normal neurological examination apart from cognitive deficits argues against Lewy body dementia, vascular dementia with focal signs, and frontotemporal dementia
Strong family history with mother having AD and brother with cognitive complaints, combined with APOE E4/E4 genotype, provides genetic context supporting the diagnosis
MRI showing bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens 3) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss is the classic structural imaging pattern for AD

Comorbid vascular risk factors (diabetes, hypertension) and Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease raise the possibility of mixed dementia, but the amnestic-predominant profile and hippocampal atrophy pattern indicate AD is the primary pathology
Normal dementia screening labs rule out treatable causes, supporting a primary neurodegenerative diagnosis

key_educational_points: Early AD classically presents with progressive episodic memory loss as the predominant symptom
Reversible causes of dementia must be systematically excluded with appropriate laboratory testing
Comorbid vascular disease is common in elderly AD patients and may contribute to cognitive decline (mixed dementia)
APOE E4/E4 homozygosity confers the highest genetic risk for late-onset AD
Hippocampal atrophy on MRI is a supportive but not pathognomonic finding for AD
CSF biomarkers (Abeta42, phospho-tau, total tau) can confirm AD pathology in vivo and are increasingly used in clinical practice
Cholinesterase inhibitors remain first-line pharmacotherapy for mild-to-moderate AD
Driving safety must be addressed when patients demonstrate navigational impairment
